  → terrific insensitivity (in social contexts)
  → terrific guts (when facing danger)

[Paraphrase chain → C]
one source ('it') → two outcomes
= different ASPECTS of the SAME character trait ✓

[Why B fails]
irreconcilable = cannot coexist, mutually exclusive
= if they were irreconcilable, they could NOT come from the same source
= but 'It makes for BOTH' = they DO coexist, they DO share a root
= B directly contradicted by 'it' making for both ✗

[The 'but also' signal — why it tempts B]
'terrific insensitivity BUT ALSO terrific guts'
= 'but also' = might suggest contrast/opposition
= but the subject is STILL 'it' (one thing)
= 'but also' = additive, not contradictory
= it adds a second outcome to the SAME source

[Character insight]
Insensitivity = he can't recognise others' difference (same trait applied to people)
Guts = he refuses to yield to danger (same trait applied to threat)
= one refusal → two faces = C ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q14): Find the pronoun 'It' — it points to ONE shared source. One source → two outcomes = same trait, different aspects (C). 'Irreconcilable' (B) requires separate sources — 'It makes for both' proves they share one. Track pronouns before choosing.

quiz.question: [Q14 — Pronoun Drill] Passage: 'He won't give in to it. It makes for terrific insensitivity but also for terrific guts.' ① 'It' refers to: the father's ________ to yield to powerlessness (one character trait) ② insensitivity = this trait applied to ________ (social/interpersonal context) ③ guts = this trait applied to ________ (danger/threat context) → One trait, two ________ = Choice C (Tap to check)
quiz.answer: ① refusal  ② people/differences  ③ danger/the mugger  → aspects
quiz.explanation:
[Full map]
'it' = the father's REFUSAL to experience powerlessness in the usual way
= one disposition, one character trait

Applied to people:
= refuses to see them as different from him
= → terrific INSENSITIVITY

Applied to danger:
= refuses to yield to the threat
= → terrific GUTS

Same refusal → two different ASPECTS depending on context
= C: different aspects of the same character trait ✓

[Why B (irreconcilable) is wrong]
If they were irreconcilable, they would come from different, incompatible sources
But they share ONE source ('it')
= they CAN coexist = not irreconcilable ✗
quiz.action: Drill habit: One 'it' → two outcomes = same trait, different aspects. Track the pronoun to find the shared source before deciding if qualities are 'same trait' or 'opposites'.

quiz.question: [Q16 — Paraphrase: What does the whole passage suggest about Philip Roth?] Passage evidence: • Lines 1–17: Philip tells the mugging story with admiration ('terrific guts') • Line 20: 'He doesn't experience powerlessness in the usual way' • Lines 23–28: Philip acknowledges father's failing: couldn't recognise difference, 'meshugge — crazy' • Line 22: 'what goes into survival isn't always pretty' Q: The whole passage suggests Philip Roth is ______. Your pick — D: resentful and bitter about his father's life-long antagonism to how he and his mother dealt with the world Correct — B: able to assess his father's strengths generously while being conscious of his father's failings Why does D's 'resentful and bitter / antagonism' overstate Philip's emotional register?
options: D is correct because Philip clearly disapproves of his father's inability to accept difference

D requires: Philip feels RESENTMENT, BITTERNESS, and sees his father as ANTAGONISTIC. But Philip tells the mugging story with admiration and humour, Joanna says 'he treats him like a son', and Philip uses 'mileage' (positive metaphor for getting value). His tone = appreciative AND critically aware. B: 'assess generously' + 'conscious of failings' = balanced, not bitter. 'Resentful/bitter/antagonism' = Too Extreme for this nuanced, affectionate-yet-honest tone.
B is wrong because Philip never explicitly praises his father
D is correct because 'meshugge' shows Philip's deep frustration with his father
quiz.answer: ② D = Too Extreme (resentful/bitter/antagonism) / B = balanced tone: generously appreciates strengths + honestly aware of failings
quiz.explanation:
[Tone audit — the whole passage]
Philip's tone is COMPLEX and BALANCED:

Positive signals:
= 'terrific guts' (admiration)
= 'He got a lot of mileage' (positive metaphor)
= tells the story to Joanna entertainingly
= no anger in his voice when recounting it

Critical signals:
= 'This is what I used to LONG FOR in him' (wished he were different)
= 'But he COULDN'T grasp it' (accepting the limitation)
= 'whoever did it different was meshugge' (notes the rigidity)

= tone: affectionate + honest = NOT resentful/bitter

[Paraphrase chain → B]
'terrific guts' + 'mileage' → assesses strengths GENEROUSLY
'what goes into survival isn't always pretty' + 'couldn't grasp it' → CONSCIOUS OF failings
= B: generous with strengths + honest about failings ✓

[Why D is Too Extreme]
resentful = deep ongoing anger
bitter = lasting pain/grievance
antagonism = hostility
= Philip DOES NOT express these
= he explains, appreciates, and accepts = not antagonistic
= D inflates mild critical observation into deep hostility ✗
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q16): Tone audit before choosing attitude choices. 'Terrific guts' + 'mileage' = generous appreciation. 'Couldn't grasp it' = honest acknowledgment of limitation. This = balanced (B), not resentful/bitter/antagonistic (D). Too Extreme indicators: resentful, bitter, antagonism.

source: Unit 127 — Q16 Logic: Why A Fails

quiz.question:
[Q16 — Logic: Why does Choice A fail?]

Choice A: 'unable to explain or come to terms with the contradictory elements of his father's nature'

Passage evidence:
• Line 23: 'what goes into survival isn't always pretty. He got a lot of mileage out of never recognising the differences...'
• Line 25–28: 'This is what I used to long for...some of her forbearance and tolerance...But he couldn't grasp it.'

Why does A misdescribe Philip as confused/unable to understand?
options: A is correct because Philip is clearly confused by his father's contradictory nature

A says Philip CANNOT explain or come to terms with his father. But Philip DOES explain perfectly: he traces both insensitivity and guts to the same survival mechanism ('mileage out of never recognising difference'). He also HAS come to terms — 'he couldn't grasp it' = acceptance of the limitation, not ongoing confusion. A demotes Philip from insightful analyst to bewildered observer.
A is wrong only because Philip doesn't use the word 'contradictory'
A is correct because the story of the mugging shows Philip still doesn't understand his father
quiz.answer: ② Philip explains the contradiction clearly AND accepts his father's limitation = A directly contradicted
quiz.explanation:
[Philip's explanation of the contradiction]
Line 23–24: 'what goes into survival isn't always pretty.
He got a lot of mileage out of never recognising the differences among people.'
= Philip EXPLAINS: both guts and insensitivity = survival mechanism
= he traces the contradiction to one root = EXPLAINS IT ✓
= A: 'unable to explain' = directly contradicted ✗

[Philip's acceptance]
Line 25–28: 'This is what I used to LONG FOR in him...
But he COULDN'T grasp it.'
= past tense 'used to long' = he has moved past the longing
= 'he couldn't grasp it' = accepting the father's limitation
= NOT ongoing confusion = has COME TO TERMS ✓
= A: 'unable to come to terms with' = directly contradicted ✗

[Philip as 'insightful analyst' not 'bewildered observer']
A makes Philip sound helpless and confused
Passage makes Philip sound: observant, analytical, accepting, nuanced
= A is a character mischaracterisation

[Combined: A eliminated on two grounds]
Ground 1: Philip DOES explain (survival = mileage from not recognising difference)
Ground 2: Philip HAS come to terms ('used to long' = past, 'couldn't grasp it' = acceptance)
quiz.action: Logic rule (Q16-A): Check if Philip DOES explain the contradiction. He does: survival mechanism = one root for both guts and insensitivity. Check if he HAS come to terms: 'used to long' (past) + 'couldn't grasp it' (acceptance) = yes. A is contradicted on both counts. B remains = balanced assessor.

source: Unit 127 — Meta: Pronoun + Tone + Too Extreme

quiz.question:
[Unit 127 — Meta Pattern]

Q14: chose B (irreconcilable opposites) — missed that 'It' = one shared source
Q16: chose D (resentful/bitter/antagonism) — Too Extreme for Philip's balanced, affectionate tone

Three tools would have prevented both errors. Which correctly identifies them?
options: Both are vocabulary errors — 'irreconcilable' and 'antagonism' were misunderstood

Tool 1 = PRONOUN TRACKING (Q14): 'It makes for both' = one source → same trait, not irreconcilable opposites. Tool 2 = TONE AUDIT (Q16): Philip's tone = generous appreciation + honest acknowledgment = balanced (B), not resentful/bitter. Tool 3 = TOO EXTREME CHECK (Q16): 'resentful', 'bitter', 'antagonism' = strong negative emotion words — check if passage actually supports this intensity. Philip: admires, uses 'mileage', accepts limitation = does NOT support these extremes.
Both are targeting errors — wrong paragraph found
Both are paraphrasing errors only — find better synonyms
quiz.answer: ② Pronoun tracking (Q14) + tone audit (Q16) + Too Extreme check (Q16) — three tools
quiz.explanation:
[Tool 1: Pronoun tracking (Q14)]
'It makes for terrific insensitivity BUT ALSO terrific guts'
'It' = one antecedent = one source
= both outcomes from ONE trait = same trait, different aspects
= 'irreconcilable' requires two separate, incompatible sources
= 'It' (one source) contradicts 'irreconcilable' (incompatible) → B eliminated

[Tool 2: Tone audit (Q16)]
Philip's tone markers:
(+) 'terrific guts' / 'mileage' / entertaining story-telling
(-) 'couldn't grasp it' / 'used to long for' / 'meshugge'
= mixed but predominantly affectionate and analytically honest
= NOT resentful, NOT bitter, NOT antagonistic

[Tool 3: Too Extreme check (Q16)]
'resentful' = deep ongoing anger → passage evidence?
'bitter' = lasting grievance → passage evidence?
'antagonism' = hostility → passage evidence?
= all three absent from Philip's actual language/tone
= whenever you see these strong negative words in a choice
= ask: does the passage EXPLICITLY support this level of negativity?
= here: No → D eliminated

[Summary]
Q14: track 'it' → one source → same trait (C)
Q16: tone audit → balanced not bitter (B) + Too Extreme check → resentful/antagonism absent (D eliminated)
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 127):
Pronoun tracking: 'it/they' pointing to one antecedent = one source = same trait, not opposites.
Tone audit: collect all positive AND negative tone markers before assigning an attitude.
Too Extreme check: 'resentful/bitter/antagonism/irreconcilable' = strong words requiring strong passage evidence. If absent = eliminate.

quiz.question: [Q89 — Paraphrase: How are relations between Mr and Mrs Pugh characterised?] Passage evidence: Surface: 'my dear', 'you know best, dear' = polite forms Below surface: rat soup, poisoners, 'brought up in pigsties', venom, 'ducks her' Q: Relations are characterised by ______. Your pick — C: stinging but ultimately innocent and polite sparring Correct — B: mutual contempt under a thin veneer of civility Why does 'innocent' in C make it immediately wrong?
options: C is correct because their arguments follow a domestic routine without real malice

'Innocent' = harmless, without malicious intent. But Mr Pugh is mentally poisoning his wife with 'a venomous porridge' that will 'scale and viper through her'. This is not innocent sparring — it is deeply hostile fantasy. B: 'veneer of civility' = the thin polite surface ('my dear') / 'mutual contempt' = the poisoning fantasy + 'pigsties' + rat soup beneath. C's 'innocent' directly contradicts the murderous internal content.
B is wrong because 'veneer' implies the civility is genuine
C is correct because outwardly their argument seems like routine bickering
quiz.answer: ② 'innocent' directly contradicted by poisoning fantasy / B = polite surface ('my dear') over deep contempt (rat soup, venom) = veneer of civility
quiz.explanation:
[Immediate contradiction test on C]
'innocent' = harmless, without malice
Mr Pugh: mentally imagines 'venomous porridge' + 'scale and viper through her' + 'ducks her in rat soup'
= these are NOT innocent thoughts
= C eliminated by contradiction ✗

[Paraphrase chain → B]
Surface layer:
'my dear' / 'you know best, dear' / 'Pigs can't read, my dear'
= formal, polite address forms
= VENEER OF CIVILITY (thin layer of politeness) ✓

Below surface:
Mr Pugh: murderous fantasies, poisons wife in imagination
Mrs Pugh: 'pigsties', bitter dust-flicking, 'trough' insult
= deep mutual hostility
= MUTUAL CONTEMPT ✓

[Combined → B]
'mutual contempt under a thin veneer of civility'
= deep hatred hidden beneath polite forms of address ✓

[The 'veneer' metaphor]
veneer = a thin attractive surface covering an uglier substance
= here: politeness ('dear') = veneer
= beneath: contempt, fantasised murder, bitterness
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q89): When a choice contains 'innocent' or similar benign qualifiers — immediately test against the passage's most extreme content. Poisoning fantasy ≠ innocent. One word ('innocent') eliminates C. Then B: veneer (polite surface) + mutual contempt (hidden hostility) = the passage's two-layer structure.

quiz.question: [Q91 — Targeting: KEY DIFFERENCE between Mr and Mrs Pugh] Mrs Pugh's expressions: • Says 'pigsties', 'trough' directly to Mr Pugh • 'Bitterly she flicks dust' (visible physical action) • Hostile comments expressed OUTWARDLY to his face Mr Pugh's expressions: • Says 'Pigs don't read, dear' / 'You know best, dear' (outwardly gentle) • Poisoning fantasy = FIRST VOICE only (internal mental state) • Anger expressed INWARDLY through imagination Q: A key difference is the degree to which they ______. Your pick — D: react to each other's comments Correct — A: externalise their reactions to each other Why does D describe a SIMILARITY and A describe the actual DIFFERENCE?
options: D is correct because Mrs Pugh reacts more strongly than Mr Pugh overall

D: both characters DO react to each other's comments — this is a similarity, not a difference. A: the DEGREE to which they EXTERNALISE is different. Mrs Pugh = externally hostile (says things out loud, physically flicks dust). Mr Pugh = internally hostile (murderous thoughts revealed only to audience via First Voice, while saying 'you know best, dear' outwardly). The difference = expression directed outward (Mrs) vs suppressed inward (Mr).
A is wrong because Mr Pugh's thoughts are not truly 'reactions' to Mrs Pugh
D is correct because Mr Pugh reacts with more physical restraint
quiz.answer: ② D = both react = similarity / A = HOW MUCH they externalise = the actual difference (Mrs=outward, Mr=inward)
quiz.explanation:
[Similarity vs Difference test]
D: 'react to each other's comments'
= both Mr and Mrs Pugh DO react
= Mrs Pugh reacts: yes (pigsties, trough, bitter flicking)
= Mr Pugh reacts: yes (internally — poisoning fantasy)
= BOTH react = D describes a SIMILARITY ✗

[The actual difference]
Mrs Pugh's reactions = EXTERNALISED:
= expressed outwardly in speech and physical action
= audience AND Mr Pugh both receive her hostility

Mr Pugh's reactions = INTERNALISED:
= expressed internally in imagination (First Voice reveals this to AUDIENCE ONLY)
= Mr Pugh says 'you know best, dear' outwardly
= he suppresses and contains his hostility

[Paraphrase chain → A]
'externalise' = express outwardly, direct outward
'degree to which they externalise' = HOW MUCH each expresses outwardly
Mrs = high degree / Mr = low degree
= different degrees of externalisation = A ✓

[The First Voice function]
First Voice = the device that reveals Mr Pugh's INTERNAL state
= without First Voice, audience would think he is merely passive
= First Voice makes the contrast visible: gentle words / violent imagination
= this is WHY the degree of externalisation is the key difference
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q91): Test each choice: does it describe a SIMILARITY or DIFFERENCE? D = both react = similarity = eliminated. A = degree of externalisation = different for each (Mrs outward / Mr inward) = the actual difference. First Voice existence = hint that Mr Pugh's reactions are internal, not external.

quiz.question: [Q92 — Targeting: Where are words and thoughts MOST OPPOSED?] Key moments: Lines 8–9: Mrs Pugh: 'Persons with manners do not read at table' (line 8) Lines 9–10: [First Voice: swallows horse-pill tablet] → more about Mrs Pugh's action Lines 12–15: Mrs Pugh: 'pigsties' / Mr Pugh: 'Pigs don't read, dear' / [First Voice: flicks dust] Line 24: Mr Pugh: 'You know best, dear' ← MOST SUBMISSIVE POSSIBLE WORDS Line 25: First Voice: 'quick as a flash he ducks her in rat soup' ← MOST VIOLENT POSSIBLE THOUGHT Q: At which point are the character's words and thoughts MOST opposed? Your pick — A: lines 8 and 9–10 Correct — C: lines 24 and 25 Why is lines 24–25 the MAXIMUM point of opposition?
options: A is correct because line 8 shows polite words while the tablet-swallowing shows discomfort

Lines 24–25 represent the MAXIMUM gap: 'You know best, dear' = ultimate submission in words (he defers completely to her judgment). Immediately after: 'quick as a flash he ducks her in rat soup' = ultimate violence in thought (drowns her in rat soup). The contrast at lines 8–10 is milder: polite criticism vs physical discomfort. 24–25 is extreme deference vs extreme violence = maximum opposition.
C is wrong because the rat soup image is not a direct reaction to 'you know best'
A is correct because the passage spends more time on lines 8–10
quiz.answer: ② Lines 24–25 = maximum opposition: extreme deference ('you know best') vs extreme violence (ducks her in rat soup) — the widest gap in the passage
quiz.explanation:
[Measuring the gap at each candidate]
Lines 8–10:
Words: 'Persons with manners do not read at table' (polite criticism)
Thoughts/action: swallows horse-pill (physical discomfort, not her thought)
= gap: mild criticism vs mild physical reaction
= moderate opposition

Lines 12–15:
Words: 'pigsties' (direct insult) / 'Pigs don't read, dear' (mild deflection)
First Voice: 'Bitterly she flicks dust' (matches her hostility)
= gap: hostile words + hostile action = internally consistent
= small opposition between words and revealed inner state

Lines 24–25: THE MAXIMUM
Words (line 24): 'You know best, dear'
= the most SUBMISSIVE, DEFERENTIAL phrase possible
= he appears completely compliant

Thought (line 25): 'quick as a flash he ducks her in rat soup'
= immediately violent fantasy
= most extreme physical violence imaginable

[Maximum opposition = C]
Extreme submission (words) ↔ Extreme violence (thought)
= widest gap between surface expression and internal state
= C ✓

[The 'quick as a flash' signal]
'Quick as a flash' = immediately, simultaneously
= the violent thought arrives WHILE he is saying the submissive words
= the two states coexist at the same moment = maximum opposition
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q92): For 'most opposed' questions — measure the GAP at each candidate point. Lines 24–25 gap: extreme submission ('you know best') ↔ extreme violence (rat soup). 'Quick as a flash' = both states simultaneous = widest gap = C. Lines 8–10 gap = mild criticism vs discomfort = smaller opposition.

quiz.question:
[Unit 126 — Meta Pattern]

Q89: chose C (innocent sparring) — 'innocent' contradicted by poisoning fantasy
Q91: chose D (react to comments) — described similarity not difference; missed externalise dimension
Q92: chose A (lines 8–10) — didn't measure the gap size; missed maximum opposition at 24–25

Three tools cover all three errors. Which correctly identifies them?
options: All three are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar dramatic terms

Tool 1 = BENIGN WORD CONTRADICTION CHECK (Q89): when a choice contains 'innocent/polite/harmless', test against the passage's most extreme content — poisoning fantasy eliminates 'innocent'. Tool 2 = SIMILARITY vs DIFFERENCE TEST (Q91): 'both react' = similarity; 'different DEGREE of externalising' = the actual difference; First Voice signals Mr Pugh's reactions are internal. Tool 3 = GAP SIZE MEASUREMENT (Q92): 'most opposed' = find the widest gap between words and thoughts — submissive words + violent thought = maximum gap at lines 24–25.
All three are targeting errors — wrong paragraph
All three are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② Tool 1 = benign word contradiction / Tool 2 = similarity vs difference + externalise / Tool 3 = gap size measurement
quiz.explanation:
[Tool 1: Benign word contradiction (Q89)]
C: 'innocent' = benign qualifier
Test against passage's most extreme content:
poisoning fantasy / rat soup / 'scale and viper through her'
= NOT innocent
= one word eliminates C immediately

Fix: When a choice contains a benign/softening qualifier (innocent, polite, harmless)
→ test against the passage's MOST extreme content
→ any extreme content = that benign word fails

[Tool 2: Similarity vs Difference (Q91)]
D: 'react to each other's comments' = BOTH react = similarity
A: 'degree to which they externalise' = HOW MUCH each expresses outward = difference
Mrs = outward / Mr = inward (First Voice reveals this)

Fix: For 'key difference' questions — test if the choice describes something BOTH do (similarity) or something that DIFFERS between them (difference)

[Tool 3: Gap size measurement (Q92)]
'Most opposed' = widest gap
Lines 8–10 gap = mild criticism vs mild discomfort = small
Lines 24–25 gap = extreme deference vs extreme violence = large

Fix: For 'most X' questions — measure and compare ALL candidates before choosing

[Dylan Thomas drama rule]
First Voice = reveals internal states not visible in speech
= creates the two-layer structure: surface words vs inner thoughts
= any question about opposition/difference = check BOTH layers
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 126):
Benign word check: 'innocent/polite/harmless' → test against most extreme passage content.
Similarity/difference: 'key difference' → verify it's not something both characters share.
Gap measurement: 'most opposed' → quantify the gap at each candidate, choose the widest.

quiz.question: [Q51 — Paraphrase: What does John Smith's breakfast dialogue show about him?] Passage (lines 1–24): • 'Over breakfast he got down to business' (straight to practical matters) • 'There must be a registry office in Katoomba, so we'll go in and get a marriage licence' • 'Do you have a birth certificate?' 'Yes, in my bag.' • 'If I can persuade the powers that be, I'd like to get it over and done with today.' Q: John Smith's dialogue indicates that he is ______. Your pick — A: devious but unsure Correct — C: pragmatic and organised How does 'got down to business' eliminate A and confirm C?
options: A is correct because John asks 'warily' showing he is uncertain about Missy's plans

'Got down to business' = immediately tackled practical matters without hesitation = NOT devious (deceptive) or unsure. John then identifies a specific office (registry), identifies a required document (birth certificate), and sets a target timeline ('today'). This sequence = PRAGMATIC (realistic, action-focused) + ORGANISED (step-by-step planning). A: 'devious' requires deceptive intent — John is entirely transparent about his intentions.
C is wrong because John shows emotion by being alarmed about a big wedding
A is correct because 'warily' shows John is suspicious of Missy's motives
quiz.answer: ② 'got down to business' = direct, no-nonsense → pragmatic / registry + birth certificate + today = step-by-step plan → organised
quiz.explanation:
['Got down to business' decoded]
= immediately addressed practical matters
= no romantic lingering, no hesitation
= ACTION-ORIENTED = pragmatic ✓

[His step-by-step sequence]
1. Identify the office: 'there must be a registry office in Katoomba'
2. Identify the document: 'Do you have a birth certificate?'
3. Set the timeline: 'get it over and done with today'
= sequential planning = ORGANISED ✓

[Why A (devious) fails]
devious = cunning, deceptive, having hidden agenda
= John's agenda is entirely transparent: get married today
= nothing hidden, nothing manipulative
= A directly contradicted by his straightforward declarations ✗

[Why A (unsure) fails]
unsure = hesitant, lacking confidence
= John states his plan as fact: 'we'll go', 'I'd like to get it done today'
= no hedging, no uncertainty in his planning
= being 'alarmed' about a big wedding = practical concern (cost/logistics)
= NOT uncertainty about the relationship ✗

[The 'warily' trap]
'What?' he asked warily (line 15)
= John is cautious about ONE specific question (burial dress = unexpected topic)
= caution about one bizarre question ≠ overall 'unsure' character trait
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q51): 'Got down to business' = pragmatic signal. Registry + birth certificate + today = organised sequence. 'Devious' requires hidden deceptive agenda (absent). 'Unsure' requires hesitation (absent — he states plans as facts). 'Warily' = one cautious moment ≠ overall character trait.

quiz.question: [Q52 — Logic: LEAST LIKELY reason Missy is embarrassed by her name?] Passage (lines 25–36): • Her reaction was 'extraordinary' — went red, shifted, clenched teeth • 'Can't you just use Missy? It's what I've always been called.' • Name = same as her house (Missalonghi) • No one calls her by real name • Father insisted on Missalonghi (loves Latin names) • Mother wanted Camilla but father 'insisted' • She wears only brown — 'poor but respectable' Q: Which is the LEAST LIKELY reason? Your pick — C: She cannot reconcile it with her current social position Correct — D: She blames her mother for not being equally insistent on Camilla Why does C remain plausible but D has no passage anchor?
options: C is the least likely because 'reconcile' is used incorrectly in the passage context

C is plausible: her social position = 'poor but respectable' with a grandiose, exotic name = hard to reconcile (mismatch is real). D requires Missy to BLAME her mother — but the passage only states the fact ('Mother wanted Camilla, he insisted') with no emotional charge toward the mother. No resentment toward mother is expressed anywhere. LEAST LIKELY = D because it invents a blame relationship absent from the passage.
D is correct because Missy clearly wishes she had been called Camilla
C is the least likely because Missy is happy with her social position
quiz.answer: ② C = plausible (grandiose name vs poor-but-respectable life = real mismatch) / D = invents blame toward mother not in passage = least likely
quiz.explanation:
[Testing each option as 'plausible reason for embarrassment']

A: 'It is the same name as her house'
= Missalonghi = name AND house = unusual, potentially embarrassing ✓ plausible

B: 'No one calls her by her real name'
= she has always been 'Missy' = real name unused = embarrassing ✓ plausible

C: 'She cannot reconcile it with her current social position'
reconcile = make compatible, bring into harmony
her position: 'poor but respectable', wears only brown, very modest life
her name: Missalonghi = grand, Latin, 'most beautiful word in the world'
= total mismatch between modest reality and grandiose name
= C is plausible as a reason ✓

D: 'She blames her mother for not being equally insistent on Camilla'
= requires: Missy feels RESENTMENT toward her mother
Passage: 'Mother wanted to call me Camilla, but he insisted on Missalonghi'
= neutral factual statement = no emotional charge toward mother
= no resentment, no blame toward mother expressed anywhere
= D invents a relationship not in passage = LEAST LIKELY ✗

[The 'least likely' logic]
Question: which is NOT a likely reason?
= find the one with LEAST or ZERO passage support
= C has genuine passage support (real mismatch)
= D has zero passage support (blame toward mother invented)
= D = least likely ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q52): For 'least likely' questions — test each option for passage support. C has real evidence (modest life + grandiose name = reconciliation problem). D requires blame toward mother — passage gives only a neutral fact. Least supported = least likely = D. Also: 'least likely' ≠ 'most wrong' — find the one with weakest passage basis.

quiz.question: [Q53 — Targeting: 'AS A WHOLE' — what is the mood of their conversation?] Overall conversation arc: • Immediately discuss practical marriage steps (registry, licence, birth certificate) • Both laugh about brown dress burial request • Missy's brown philosophy explained and accepted ('That made him laugh') • 'Missalonghi' name revealed → 'He burst out laughing' • 'You poor little bitch!' = affectionate response in laughing context Q: Their conversation, AS A WHOLE, shows a mood of ______. Your pick — A: distrust and suspicion Correct — C: acceptance and contentment Which words in the passage are the 'as a whole' targets, and which are the local distractors?
options: A is correct because 'warily' and 'alarmed' show both characters are on guard

'As a whole' requires the dominant mood across the whole conversation, not isolated moments. DISTRACTORS: 'warily' (1 moment) + 'alarmed' (1 moment) = local reactions to specific surprises, not the overall tone. OVERALL EVIDENCE: both laugh repeatedly, she explains her quirks openly, he accepts them ('made him laugh'), they proceed cheerfully toward marriage. C: acceptance = each accepts the other's oddities / contentment = they are comfortable moving forward together.
C is wrong because 'You poor little bitch!' shows hostility
A is correct because they are still getting to know each other and therefore suspicious
quiz.answer: ② 'warily'/'alarmed' = local distractor moments / repeated laughter + mutual acceptance of quirks = overall mood = C
quiz.explanation:
['As a whole' discipline — collect all mood evidence]

Local distractor moments (A's evidence):
'warily' (line 15) = one cautious question moment
'alarmed' (line 7) = one surprised moment about big wedding
= these are REACTIONS TO SPECIFIC SURPRISES, not the overall mood

Overall mood evidence (C's evidence):
'That made him laugh' (line 21) = accepts her brown philosophy with warmth
'He burst out laughing' (line 31) = delighted by her name
'You poor little bitch!' (line 36) = affectionate in this laughing context
Practical marriage planning = contentment with their decision
= acceptance: each takes the other's quirks in stride
= contentment: they are moving happily toward marriage

[Why A (distrust/suspicion) fails the whole-passage test]
distrust = ongoing doubt about the other's motives
= John's actions: moves quickly toward marriage = NOT distrustful
Missy's actions: openly shares embarrassing name, quirky burial request
= NOT guarded or suspicious
= two local reaction words ('warily', 'alarmed') ≠ sustained distrust throughout

[The 'as a whole' instruction]
= do not anchor to one or two local words
= survey the dominant emotional register across the whole exchange
= dominant register here: warmth, laughter, mutual acceptance = C ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q53): 'As a whole' = survey the DOMINANT mood across the entire passage. Identify local distractor moments ('warily', 'alarmed') vs overall pattern (repeated laughter, mutual acceptance, forward movement to marriage). Dominant pattern = acceptance and contentment = C. Two cautious words ≠ overall distrust.

quiz.question:
[Unit 125 — Meta Pattern]

Q51: chose A (devious/unsure) — missed 'got down to business' = pragmatic signal
Q52: chose C (cannot reconcile) — it IS a plausible reason; D (blame mother) has no anchor
Q53: chose A (distrust/suspicion) — anchored to local words ('warily', 'alarmed') not overall mood

Three distinct question-type fixes. Which correctly identifies them?
options: All three are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar words caused misreading

Q51 fix = FIRST-SENTENCE ANCHOR: 'got down to business' = pragmatic signal that should lock the character reading (pragmatic + organised). Q52 fix = LEAST-LIKELY LOGIC: test each option for passage support — C has real mismatch evidence; D invents blame with zero anchor = least supported = least likely. Q53 fix = AS-A-WHOLE DISCIPLINE: collect dominant mood evidence, not local reaction words — 'warily' + 'alarmed' = local; repeated laughter + acceptance = dominant.
All three are targeting errors — wrong paragraph
All three are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② Q51 = first-sentence anchor (pragmatic) / Q52 = least-likely = least supported / Q53 = dominant mood not local words
quiz.explanation:
[Q51: First-sentence anchor]
'Over breakfast he got down to business'
= first sentence sets the character register
= 'got down to business' = pragmatic, direct, no-nonsense
= all subsequent actions (registry, birth certificate, today) = organised
= lock this reading from line 1

[Q52: Least-likely logic]
Step 1: test each option against passage evidence
A = plausible (same name as house) ✓
B = plausible (no one uses real name) ✓
C = plausible (grandiose name vs modest life = mismatch) ✓
D = NOT supported (blame toward mother = invented) ✗
Step 2: least supported = least likely = D

[Q53: As-a-whole discipline]
Step 1: identify LOCAL reaction words: warily, alarmed
Step 2: identify OVERALL pattern: laugh, laugh, laugh, acceptance, forward movement
Step 3: dominant pattern wins for 'as a whole' questions
= C: acceptance and contentment ✓

[Three question-type rules]
'Character X's dialogue indicates he is' → anchor to first action + consistent pattern
'Least likely reason' → test ALL options for support; choose least supported
'As a whole, mood is' → dominant pattern, not isolated local words
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 125):
Character questions: first action sets the register ('got down to business' = pragmatic).
Least likely: test each option for passage support systematically; D has zero support = least likely.
As a whole: suppress local distractors ('warily') and find the dominant emotional pattern across the full passage.

quiz.question: [Q27 — Vocab: How would an actor speak lines 31–32?] Context: Stephanie (lines 29–30): criticises Marcus's job as 'meaningless, fraudulent, exploitative' Marcus (lines 31–32): 'I suppose you think teaching semiotics is holding the fabric of the universe together' = Marcus mocks Stephanie's high opinion of her OWN former work by deliberately OVERSTATING its cosmic importance Q: An actor would most likely speak these lines ______. Your pick — D: with bewilderment Correct — B: sarcastically Why does the context eliminate D and demand B?
options: D is correct because Marcus is genuinely confused by Stephanie's logic

Bewilderment = genuine confusion/disorientation. But Marcus is COUNTER-ATTACKING — he has just been attacked and is now mocking Stephanie's self-importance with deliberate exaggeration ('holding the FABRIC OF THE UNIVERSE together' = absurd overstatement). This is classic sarcasm: saying the grandiose opposite of what you mean to mock someone. D would require Marcus not to understand what's happening — he understands perfectly and is hitting back.
B is wrong because Marcus is not shown to be generally sarcastic in the play
D is correct because Marcus is surprised by Stephanie's sudden attack on his job
quiz.answer: ② bewilderment = confusion / Marcus is counter-attacking with deliberate overstatement = sarcasm (B)
quiz.explanation:
[Sarcasm mechanics decoded]
Sarcasm = saying the grandiose or exaggerated OPPOSITE of what you mean
= to mock someone's inflated self-image

Marcus's line: 'I suppose you think teaching semiotics is holding the FABRIC OF THE UNIVERSE TOGETHER'
= 'fabric of the universe' = cosmically important language
= applied to academic semiotics = absurdly overblown
= Marcus knows Stephanie does NOT think this
= he is MOCKING her implied self-importance

[Why D (bewilderment) fails]
bewilderment = lost, confused, not understanding
= if Marcus were bewildered: 'What? Why are you attacking me? I don't understand'
= but Marcus DOES understand → he is counter-attacking intelligently
= bewilderment requires cognitive confusion, not strategic mockery

[The counter-attack structure]
Stephanie: 'your job is fraudulent/exploitative'
Marcus: 'I suppose YOUR job was saving the universe' (sarcastic exaggeration)
= reciprocal mockery = sarcasm ✓

[Vocab precision]
sarcastically = mockingly, with ironic exaggeration
bewilderment = genuine confusion about what is happening
indulgently = with tolerant amusement
with curiosity = wanting to understand more
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q27): 'Fabric of the universe' = deliberate cosmic overstatement to mock Stephanie's self-importance = sarcasm. Bewilderment requires genuine confusion — Marcus is counter-attacking strategically. Learn: sarcasm = deliberate exaggeration/inversion to mock. Bewilderment = lost/confused.

quiz.question: [Q28 — Targeting: Marcus's impression about selling the house] Passage (lines 8–9): Marcus: 'I couldn't take it on... I wasn't there long enough. It would have been too much to ask of them so soon.' ← Stephanie did NOT ask him to explain ← He volunteers this defence unprompted Q: Concerning selling the house, Marcus gives the impression that he ______. Your pick — A: feels it was nothing to do with him Correct — C: feels apologetic towards Stephanie because he didn't do anything to help Why does volunteering an unprompted defence signal apologetic feelings?
options: A is correct because Marcus is a real estate agent and selling houses is just his job

A (nothing to do with him) would mean Marcus doesn't feel responsible — but if it were nothing to do with him, he would NOT bother explaining himself. The fact that he VOLUNTEERS a defence without being asked = guilty conscience = apologetic. C: he is a real estate agent who SHOULD have helped sell the family house, didn't, and now justifies himself pre-emptively = apologetic.
C is wrong because Marcus never explicitly says he is sorry
A is correct because Stephanie already says she managed fine, showing Marcus wasn't needed
quiz.answer: ② Unprompted defence = guilty conscience = apologetic (C) / 'nothing to do with him' would mean no defence needed
quiz.explanation:
[The unprompted defence signal]
Stephanie: 'Even me.' (line 5) — neutral, just mentioning she sold a house
Marcus: immediately launches into defence ('I couldn't take it on... It would have been too much...')
= Stephanie did NOT accuse him
= he brings up his own failure and justifies it
= PROACTIVE DEFENCE = guilty conscience = apologetic ✓

[Why A is self-contradicting]
If Marcus felt it was 'nothing to do with him':
= he would not mention it
= he would not justify himself
= there would be no need for a defence
But he DOES defend himself unprompted
= therefore he DOES feel it was to do with him
= A directly contradicted by his own behaviour ✗

[The occupational dimension]
Marcus = real estate agent
Family house needed selling
= the obvious person to help = him
= he didn't help
= he feels the weight of that obligation he didn't fulfil
= C: apologetic because he didn't do anything to help ✓

[Behavioural logic]
Unprompted defence = I feel I owe an explanation
= I feel I did something wrong
= apologetic feelings ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q28): Unprompted defence = guilty conscience = apologetic (C). When a character justifies themselves before being accused, they feel responsible. A requires indifference — but his defence proves the opposite. Real estate agent + family house unsold = obvious unfulfilled obligation.

quiz.question: [Q29 — Targeting: Stephanie's impression about selling the house] Passage (lines 13–15): Marcus: 'I'm glad you don't mind.' Stephanie: 'Of course I mind, you jerk. Of course I mind. It's bad enough you work in such a cruddy job, but you can't even do something useful with it when it comes to the crunch, of course I mind.' Q: Concerning selling the house, Stephanie gives the impression that ______. Your pick — B: it was a depressing and gruelling task, and she is glad it is over Correct — A: she resents the fact that Marcus did not offer any help What is Stephanie's anger DIRECTED AT?
options: B is correct because selling a house during a father's terminal illness is objectively depressing

B describes Stephanie's feelings about the TASK itself. But her explosion is directed at MARCUS specifically: 'you jerk', 'you can't even do something useful with it when it comes to the crunch'. The word 'you' appears repeatedly — her anger is directed at HIS FAILURE to help, not at the difficulty of the task. A: she resents that Marcus didn't offer help = directly anchored in lines 13–15.
A is wrong because Stephanie says 'I managed' showing she doesn't need Marcus
B is correct because 'Of course I mind' shows she found the experience painful
quiz.answer: ② B = about the task / A = about Marcus's failure to help — 'you jerk', 'you can't even do something useful' = anger directed at HIM specifically
quiz.explanation:
[Direction of Stephanie's anger]
'Of course I mind, YOU jerk'
'YOU can't even do something useful with it'
'when it comes to the crunch' = when it really mattered

= all pronouns point to MARCUS
= anger is directed at HIS FAILURE, not at the task's difficulty

[Why B is misdirected]
B: 'depressing and gruelling task, glad it is over'
= about the difficulty OF SELLING THE HOUSE
= but Stephanie doesn't describe the task as depressing
= she describes Marcus as useless when she needed him
= her emotion = resentment toward Marcus, not relief about task ending

[Paraphrase chain → A]
'You can't even do something useful with it when it comes to the crunch'
= when she needed practical help (crunch time), he provided nothing
= she RESENTS this failure
= A: 'resents the fact that Marcus did not offer any help' ✓

[The 'jerk' signal]
'Of course I mind, you JERK'
= personal insult directed at Marcus
= emotional charge = resentment/anger at a PERSON
= NOT relief that a task is over (which would be calm, not angry)
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q29): Check WHO Stephanie's anger is directed at. 'You jerk' + 'you can't even' = anger at MARCUS specifically. B is about the task (wrong direction). A = resents Marcus's failure = correct direction. Track pronouns in emotional outbursts — they reveal the target of the emotion.

quiz.question: [Q30 — Targeting: Why does Marcus laugh (line 25)?] Context: Stephanie (lines 21–23): 'A complete waste. Opening front doors for suburban slime... It's demeaning. It's menial.' = detailed, forceful, multi-word attack on his profession Marcus (line 24): 'I enjoy it. I get to travel and meet people.' [He laughs.] (line 25) Q: Why does Marcus laugh? Your pick — B: He doesn't really mean what he is saying Correct — D: He recognises that what he is saying sounds pretty weak Why is D the correct reading of this laughter?
options: B is correct because Marcus is just defending himself politely without conviction

B requires Marcus to be insincere — but he probably DOES enjoy his job. D: Stephanie has just deployed a sophisticated, multi-layered attack ('suburban slime, demeaning, menial'). Marcus's response: 'I enjoy it. I get to travel and meet people.' = thin, simple defence against a powerful argument. He laughs because he RECOGNISES his own defence sounds hollow in comparison — self-aware acknowledgment of a weak counter-argument.
D is wrong because Marcus never explicitly says he thinks his argument is weak
B is correct because 'I get to travel and meet people' is too casual to be his real view
quiz.answer: ② D = self-aware laugh at the weakness of his own defence / B requires insincerity (he probably genuinely enjoys his job)
quiz.explanation:
[The mismatch that causes the laugh]
Stephanie's attack: elaborate, specific, multi-word
= 'suburban slime' / 'demeaning' / 'menial' / 'complete waste'
= rhetorically powerful

Marcus's defence: simple, generic
= 'I enjoy it. I get to travel and meet people.'
= thin, barely addresses her specific points

[The laugh = self-awareness]
He laughs = he HEARS his own words as they come out
= he recognises the gap between her attack and his response
= D: 'recognises that what he is saying sounds pretty weak' ✓

[Why B (not sincere) is wrong]
B requires: Marcus doesn't actually enjoy his job
= but nothing in the passage suggests he dislikes it
= he later defends it further ('It's important. People need real estate agents.')
= he seems to genuinely believe in its value
= B invents insincerity not in the text ✗

[Laughter as self-aware acknowledgment]
In drama, a character laughing at their own words often signals:
= 'even I can hear how weak this sounds'
= not lying, just outgunned in the debate
= D = self-aware humour about the inadequacy of his counter-argument ✓
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q30): Stephanie's attack = sophisticated and multi-layered. Marcus's defence = simple and thin. [He laughs] = self-awareness that his response sounds weak compared to her attack = D. B requires insincerity — he genuinely enjoys his job (defends it again later). Laughter after a thin defence = D.

quiz.question: [Q32 — Paraphrase: How does Stephanie feel about Marcus and his way of life?] Passage (lines 18–19): 'It's a waste.' [pause] 'It's a waste of you, your life.' Q: Which best describes Stephanie's feelings about her brother? Your pick — A: She thinks he is worthless and has no potential Correct — B: She is angry with him because he is not living up to her expectations How does 'waste of YOU' vs 'worthless' reveal a critical distinction?
options: A is correct because Stephanie calls his job 'meaningless, fraudulent, exploitative'

'Waste of YOU' = YOU have value that is being wasted. If Marcus were worthless (A), there would be NOTHING to waste. 'Waste' logically requires the existence of something valuable being squandered. Stephanie sees Marcus as having POTENTIAL she believes he is not using. B: 'not living up to her expectations' = she expects more from him because she believes he is capable of more = the exact logic of 'waste'.
B is wrong because Stephanie never explicitly states her expectations for Marcus
A is correct because 'fraudulent and exploitative' shows she has no respect for him
quiz.answer: ② 'waste of YOU' = YOU have value being squandered / worthless = no value to waste = logically impossible combination
quiz.explanation:
[The logical structure of 'waste']
waste (noun) = using something valuable in a way that doesn't honour its value
= waste REQUIRES value to exist in the first place

'It's a waste of YOU'
= YOU = valuable person
= being wasted = not using that value properly
= Stephanie believes Marcus HAS something worth more than this job

[Why A is logically contradicted]
A: 'worthless, has no potential'
= if he has NO value, there is NOTHING to waste
= you cannot waste something worthless
= 'waste of you' REQUIRES the opposite of worthless
= A is a logical impossibility given the word 'waste' ✗

[Paraphrase chain → B]
'waste of you' = you have potential not being used
= she EXPECTS more from him based on that potential
= he is NOT meeting those expectations
= B: 'not living up to her expectations' ✓

[The anger source]
If Marcus were worthless (A): she might feel contempt or resignation
If he is wasting his potential (B): she feels FRUSTRATION and ANGER
= anger requires believing the person COULD DO BETTER
= Stephanie's passionate outburst = frustrated expectations, not contempt
quiz.action: Action Rule (Q32): 'Waste of you' = logical structure: waste requires VALUE to exist. Therefore she thinks Marcus has value/potential. A (worthless) = logically impossible with 'waste'. B (not living up to expectations) = she believes he is capable of more = the meaning of 'waste of you'. Waste ≠ worthless.

source: Unit 124 — Meta: Sarcasm + Behavioural Logic + Word Logic

quiz.question:
[Unit 124 — Meta Pattern]

Q27: chose D (bewilderment) — didn't know 'sarcastically'
Q28: chose A (nothing to do with him) — missed unprompted defence = guilty conscience
Q29: chose B (task was hard) — missed anger directed at Marcus specifically
Q30: chose B (not sincere) — missed self-aware laugh at weak defence
Q32: chose A (worthless) — missed logical structure of 'waste'

Three correction principles cover all five. Which correctly identifies them?
options: All five are vocabulary errors — unfamiliar words

P1 = VOCAB+CONTEXT (Q27): 'sarcastically' = deliberate exaggeration to mock; 'bewilderment' = confusion — context of counter-attack demands sarcasm. P2 = BEHAVIOURAL LOGIC (Q28, Q29, Q30): unprompted defence = guilty/apologetic; anger with 'you jerk/you can't' = directed at Marcus not the task; self-aware laugh after thin defence = recognises weakness (D). P3 = WORD LOGIC (Q32): 'waste of you' logically requires Marcus to have VALUE = not worthless = B.
All three are targeting errors — wrong paragraph
All five are paraphrasing errors only
quiz.answer: ② P1 = vocab+context (sarcasm vs bewilderment) / P2 = behavioural logic (defence=apologetic, you=direction, laugh=self-aware) / P3 = word logic (waste requires value)
quiz.explanation:
[P1: Vocab + Context (Q27)]
sarcastically = deliberate overstatement/inversion to mock
bewilderment = genuine confusion about what's happening
Context: Marcus is counter-attacking = strategic, not confused
→ sarcastically ✓

[P2: Behavioural Logic (Q28, Q29, Q30)]
Q28: volunteering unprompted defence → feels responsible → apologetic (C)
Q29: 'you jerk' + 'you can't' → anger directed at Marcus (not the task) → resents his failure (A)
Q30: thin defence after powerful attack + [laughs] → self-aware about weakness → D

Behavioural logic = CHARACTER'S ACTIONS reveal their internal state
unprompted = guilty / anger at 'you' = at him / laugh after weak response = self-aware

[P3: Word Logic (Q32)]
'waste of you' = logical structure:
→ waste requires value to exist
→ 'of you' = you are what is being wasted
→ you HAVE value being squandered
→ NOT worthless (A) = impossible
→ not meeting potential (B) = correct ✓

Fix: Check the logical structure of key words
waste = something valuable used poorly
worthless = no value
logically incompatible when said about the same person
quiz.action:
Meta Rule (Unit 124):
Vocab+Context: sarcasm = deliberate exaggeration to mock (not confusion).
Behavioural logic: unprompted=guilty; 'you' pronoun=direction of anger; self-aware laugh=recognises weakness.
Word logic: 'waste of you' = you have value = not worthless = expectations not met.
